title: Good Pay, Variable Work/Life Balance, Bad Culture
body: Good Pay, Variable Work/Life Balance, Bad Culture
pros: The pay was good and the people I worked with were great!
cons: The work/life balance really depends on the role that you get. If you're more tied to manufacturing/pilot plant, you're on call and have daily meetings (before 8 AM AND after 4 PM) making it hard to do anything Although we have flextime, it does bleed into personal life and it's hard to manage sometimes. The lab folks had pretty laid back schedules and workloads. The forced ranking system is archaic and causes a lot of distrust in management. Also management should not control your career prospects. It's a really toxic system. I've also seen a lot of microaggressions in the plant and racist comments by operators which made me feel unwelcome.

title: Overall ExxonMobil is a a good place to work and take care of your family
body: Overall ExxonMobil is a a good place to work and take care of your family
pros: Decent pay. Good benefits. Mostly good coworkers. Pension. On a 12-hour shift, you get paid 8 hours of straight time and 4 hours of overtime, so your pay averages out to be more. If you work in unscheduled overtime, it's paid at a rate of 1.71 (instead of 1.5), and double time days are paid at a rate of 2.28 (instead of 2.0). You get paid for holidays regardless of if you work or not, but if you do work a holiday, you get paid all premium pay.
cons: Union facility, A lot of bad attitudes, always feel like your job is being threatened, 4/4 shift schedule (new schedule was voted on, supposed to start in 2026. 5/4/4/5 shift schedule.)

title: Can Be Great If You Actively Play "The Game"
body: Can Be Great If You Actively Play "The Game"
pros: â¢ Great salary â¢ Innovative ideas and solutions are highly valued â¢ Clean â¢ Safe â¢ Modern â¢ Spring, TX campus is beautiful â¢ Active in community volunteering â¢ This is an environment that values coming in early and leaving late with "butts in seats", not working from home.

title: Awful Upper Management
pros: Some of the managers and employees I've worked with are great, and the pay isn't awful for the location.
cons: Upper management bullies their managers and employees, berating them in front of customer and coworkers, treats their CSRs like 2nd class citizens, and basically do as they please. They'll work you to death, add THEIR work on top of it, then get mad when it's not done how they would do it. They offer bonuses and vacation days, but out right refuse to give them to you or set up new stipulations to prevent you from getting them as well. That's just the surface of it too. First time I've ever been written up and screamed at because we sold out of something and had to wait on our truck to deliver more. I ended up quitting after one of our employees got fired for telling corporate about a DM that never showed up and did their job, only for her to get caught and them ask why we never said anything. I quit when they demanded I write up a good employee for not coming into work because they we're in the hospital. Lazy and irresponsible people in charge.

title: Avoid if you value growth
pros: Pay, benefits, and the campus is nice if you like how hospitals feel and no privacy.
cons: If you're a creative professional looking for a supportive and collaborative workplace, look elsewhere. This company fosters a fear-based work culture through its toxic ranking system, which pits employees against one another, including against their own managers. Thereâs a reason why performance assessments are jokingly referred to as âThe Hunger Gamesâ among employees. Leadership thrives on ambiguity, and many managers will either neglect you entirely, refuse to delegate meaningful work, or actively sabotage you by speaking out of both sides of their mouth. Accountability is nonexistent, and favoritism runs rampant. That said, having this company on your resume may open doors elsewhereâbut the toll it takes on your well-being may not be worth it. Proceed with caution.

title: Excellent people, organization in perpetual transition
body: Excellent people, organization in perpetual transition
pros: ExxonMobil has historically had an uncanny ability of attracting top-tier talent in the engineering fields. All of my colleagues are top tier, with very few exceptions. Workplace flexibility has significantly improved over the past several years. Compensation has recovered from the lows experienced over COVID. This is particularly true for top ranked employees. The management culture has a very strong emphasis on controls, which results in a very high ethical standard.
cons: Over the past several years the organization feels like itâs in a constant state of change, which distracts from people focusing on the bottom line. The organization is strongly tilted towards management, with less of a focus on leadership, resulting in change efforts, often being over managed and under inspiring. We are opportunities for employees in âhigh cost 10â countries are shrinking. Many white collar jobs from the US, Europe, and Australia, are being exported to India.

title: Thereâs a hole in the bucket!
body: Thereâs a hole in the bucket!
pros: I started working here after a hotshot from Exxon went up to the drive thru that I worked at when I was 16. He ordered a burger, no tomato cause only wusses donât eat tomatoâs, but what do I know?!?! Anyways, he gets a burger, he is on his hardwired cellular phone in his car, takes a big bite of his burger, and glances over at me, where my jaw is dropped as I wait for him to take the change from my hand thatâs been extended for a minute, and he says âkeep the change eh? You need it if you are gonna work for me someday!â Tomorrow, 8am sharp! Bring a mop!â So I got the job. I didnât tell him I only worked the cashier and I didnât make his burger. Oh well.
cons: Every time I turn around somebody needs something! It can be annoying. Oh, there was this one time, they brought some barrels of oil in, and I happened to be mopping around them, and my buddy sends me a text, but while reaching for my phone, the mop drops and punctures a hole in the side of the barrel. My boss saw me pull the mop out, and called me an idiot. He made me plug the hole with my mop, but I thought my finger would make more sense. Well, I waited there all day until they got someone in to drain the barrel. I ended up missing my lunch.

pros: The company is very good in educating its employees and make them experts in their fields. They pay a good salary too.
cons: Very high load of work. No bonus. The worst part is the ranking system they have for annual performance evaluations. They ranks the coworkers in similar teams against each other and every year they have to put 5-10% of people in the âneeds improvementâ category, even if all the tram members did great in that year and met all the requirements. title: Good Initial Experience Builder and for Experienced Hires with Families
body: Good Initial Experience Builder and for Experienced Hires with Families
pros: Opportunity to take on a lot of responsibility early on. Competitive starting salary and compensation package. Great 401K match. Medical benefits for the whole family available.
cons: Once you have joined the company, base pay can become uncompetitive and the whole compensation package is what maintains competitiveness. However, the pension part of the package is lost if you leave the company before retirement. Flex schedule is advertised but depends on your management, with many supervisors requiring employees to be in the office every day even if your team is overseas and calls are on Zoom. Culture can be negative on some teams due to high turnover. You can get stuck in an area of the company, losing the advertised movement and growth potential. This can occur due to turnover, requiring analysts who have stayed to train new hires as turnover continues.

title: Good and Bad about ExxonMobil
pros: Opportunities to travel domestically and internationally, lots of smart people, top technology, above average salaries, lots of opportunities to learn
cons: Politics within teams and the department, 3 classes of people at the Company: Top performers (who are identified by management many times who are not top performers), most people fall into average performers even if they are keeping the most critical processes and systems working, low performers who get this designation because they don't play politics or step on someone's toes. Nearly everyone at the company works hard so each year at ranking/evaluation time a large chunk still get classified as low performers and the reasons for the classification for many people may have never been discussed ahead of time. The company allows limited flexibility working remotely but wants people in the office. The company is always looking to save money and move jobs to cheaper locations.

title: Oh, how the mighty have fallen
body: Oh, how the mighty have fallen
pros: Note: from an IT employeeâs perspective EMâs scale, sheer complexity, and balance sheet still offer massive opportunity to learn and work with fairly modern systems and technology for a fair number of its workers. For a select few that are either tagged as âexecutive potentialâ or those with personal drive/motivation to âgrow and move onâ, ExxonMobil IT offers a wealth of opportunity.
cons: Note: from an IT employeeâs perspective Years of brain drain, consisting of many talented employees at all levels (up to and including executive ranks) globally, coupled with a corporate mandate to offshore and outsource IT have left the organization crippled and on the verge of system failures on the same level as operating incidents already experienced in ExxonMobilâs business units over the past few years.

title: Good Opportunities but People Processes Suck
body: Good Opportunities but People Processes Suck
pros: Lots of opportunities to develop, high level of responsibility, generally good people to work with. Work/life balance is good as long as you donât work at a manufacturing site.
cons: All of the HR processes are horrible. I was actually assessed (ranked) fairly well but the raises were bad. I felt bad for my peers who werenât ranked as well but did equal quality work (you need a certain personality to get ranked wellâ¦ relating to management is key). Dallas (corporate) is too removed from the worker bees and unilaterally makes decisions. I guess with Exxon trying to cut headcount it makes sense that they didnât really value their people. I left and am not getting paid 80k more. Everyone is burnt out and jaded at Exxon.

title: Morale Declining, Management is Key
pros: I have major flexibility and minimal micromanagement in my current role but that is SOLELY because of my supervisor and if that position changes to a new person it could go away. It also helps/doesn't help that my supervisor is not involved in my day to day at all so they have no idea what I do all day; great for no micromanaging, not great when it comes to advocating for me in performance reviews. Variety of experience, able to work through turnarounds, improvement opportunities, variety of equipment types, leadership/management DO want to develop you to exent and take initiative to move you around and put you in different experience opportunities.
cons: Higher level leadership is clearly attempting to reduce headcount in the engineering resources area to save money but advertising it as âeliminating unnecessary tasksâ by allowing engineers to focus on bigger picture items and sending those smaller responsibilities to overseas tech centers but that isnât really happening as engineers are still spending alot of time on stewardship activities and repeat communication to multiple âsupervisorâ type roles throughout the business units the engineers support. Morale is low as people are quitting or "escaping" to other parts of the company (outside of manufacturing) everyday. Others in similar roles to me are unable to work from home ever and have minimal flexibility so it just depends.

title: Competitive Intern Experience
pros: All of the employees at ExxonMobil are brilliant and personable individuals. You can learn so much information from senior engineers. The work can be demanding and challenging, but I think this could fit some individuals' lifestyles.
cons: The work environment at ExxonMobil claims to be collaborative and team-oriented. However, the corporate structure itself does little to promote this type of behavior. In its place is a system that rewards the work and achievements of individuals. As a result, the workplace fails to promote the nurturing culture it dreams of and instead becomes competitive at every level from interns, engineers, and senior leadership.

title: Good Work Life Balance if People in Your Group Didn't All Quit
body: Good Work Life Balance if People in Your Group Didn't All Quit
pros: Great pay for a civil engineer that can't be beat working for traditional civil engineering companies. Good work/life balance (most people get into the office by around 8am, and the parking garage is cleared out by 5pm). Good flexibility in working remotely if needed for doctor appointments, etc. Great networking opportunities within the company and you can have fun playing the corporate "game." You get a new position every 2-3 years so never get pigeon holed into doing only 1 thing.
cons: Everybody in my group quit (a trend these days), so my hours have been closer to 12+ hr days. Honestly I'm just not passionate about oil and gas. The pay can't be beat if you're a civil engineer, which is why I decided to give the job a chance. I'm actively looking for jobs elsewhere now because I can't scratch the itch for wanting to work on a civil infrastructure or building construction project (the reason I chose to get my degree in civil engineering).

title: Popularity based ranking, terrible corporate management, technical malfeasance
body: Popularity based ranking, terrible corporate management, technical malfeasance
pros: A lot of good challenging work in plant environment. Work life balance is what you'd expect from plant work. If you don't expect to work weekends and nights, you probably shouldn't work in a plant.
cons: Corporation has poor vision and leadership. They overhired before COVID and then began to pretty much wage war on their employees. There has been a push to outsource engineering work overseas so much to the point that work from US based personnel are being credited to the overseas technical centers so management can make the result match the goal. Technical careers are not valued. During the COVID downturn, working people were slashed and management headcount seemed to increase, where I was working. Too many chiefs, not enough indians.

title: Great for technical training, but sad and cold environment for Contact Engineers
body: Great for technical training, but sad and cold environment for Contact Engineers
pros: Salary was great! It was the highest offer I got. Training was top notch. I was groomed to be a senior technical in a refining technology. The brand is respected on a resume. Also, you work with some of the sharpest people in the game. Very structured training, practices and procedures.
cons: If youâre a contact your will work long hours and be stressed. Cold and impersonal environment. The ranking system still exists but it is called assessment groups or something now, same forces ranking. You will be ranked amongst your peers so the environment is sort of a pressure cooker at all times. You do not control your career. You go where you are needed. When things are bad, the bottom 10% gets laid off. Anyone can be in the bottom 10% regardless of your performance, it is a combination of luck, experience, who likes you, your group, what good project falls on your lap, etc. if you are less than 5 years, and youâre in the bottom 20%, then youâre out.

title: Overall desirable company for chemical engineers.
body: Overall desirable company for chemical engineers.
pros: The work is very interesting (bread and butter chemical engineering) in a fast-paced environment. Employees who are willing to put in long hours and stay loyal to the company will be rewarded with long-term advancement, and being that it is a very large company, there are many different career paths one can take. Pay and benefits are pretty good.
cons: Work life balance can be difficult working at the site level. Engineers are on-call 24/7 and many times must schedule to have a backfill on the weekends whenever they travel within a larger radius than 2 hours from the plant. Morale post-COVID has been pretty bad with very high attrition, but it has seemed to improve with increasing company profitability in 2022.

title: Is the salary worth the hell?
body: Is the salary worth the hell?
pros: Great salary Great community You will learn a lot, quickly
cons: Antiquated technology - lots of my work was on programming and maintaining PLCs from the early 90's On call 24/7 - you will get called to the field in the middle of the night, on weekends, on holidays, and while you're on a date. You will not get compensated for these calls. No mileage reimbursement - I drove over 13,000 miles for this company without being reimbursed Unappreciated work - as an ELECTRICAL field engineer specifically, your job will essentially be a glorified electrical technician. No matter how many 10pm nights you put in, the technical innovations you come up with, or the the millions of dollars of revenue you generate, you will always be out ranked by the project managers and the business team. Sad but true

title: Too much fat at the top
body: Too much fat at the top
pros: '- Above average pay in Houston and in the industry
cons: '- If you are in IT, pay is below average comparing to tech companies of the same size - Too much fat at the top in the IT org: senior management and leadership are clueless and mostly non-technical. Decision making from the leadership is poor and often for shows and power grabbing, not for the good will of the company. - Morality is extremely low. Regular developers suffer the most from bad leadership.

title: Predictable old-school oil and gas
pros: High salary Good place to get experience early in career Great opportunities for advancement if you're on the management track, a "Campus Hire" (hired out of school), and have a trailing spouse (and so are willing to move anywhere at any time)

title: Rating and Ranking - Cyclical and dying industry - not recommended long term
body: Rating and Ranking - Cyclical and dying industry - not recommended long term
pros: Top 500 corporation - a great start for your career/resume from year 1-5.
cons: Long term - you are simply an employee number at the end of the day. The closer you get to retirement your ranking will drop and it will be convenient to let you go before reaching retirement. Worrying about your employment status every few years due to the industry. If they over extend in oil exploration, they apply the same layoff standard to all sides of the business no matter how profitable your team is. Highly recommend you avoid this corporation if you have a family and a good heart that truly cares about people. Moved all customer service jobs to Buenos Aires - does not care about employing USA - only cares about pennies on the dollar no matter where they can get it. Look at Shell and Chevron before ExxonMobil as they allow their employees to interview for other jobs in the company before laying them off. If you are looking for a sales position they are moving away from outside sales to support customers to save more $$.

title: Great pay and benefits, lots of politics
body: Great pay and benefits, lots of politics
pros: Exxon pays a premium to competitors. Large company has lots of career progression opportunities. Nice facilities and perks.
cons: Incredibly political. Very slow to change or adapt. High turnover in the last 3-4 years with big layoffs and employees leaving. Major reputational issues. Poor employee relations. HR surprisingly unprofessional for a large company.

title: Toxic management, poor compensation, and arbitrary performance review system
body: Toxic management, poor compensation, and arbitrary performance review system
pros: A chance to work in high-interest R&D. Great research freedom and funding. Advancement opportunities if you play the game
cons: Toxic management culture. No feedback, completely opaque and subjective performance review process, despite numerous claims otherwise. Many were encouraged and told they were doing great, then fired for 'poor performance' the next week. 401k match taken away for nearly 2 years. Terrible morale for both union/nonunion workers. Pervasive sense of dishonesty in all management decisions.

title: When things get bad, forced ranking makes it worse
body: When things get bad, forced ranking makes it worse
pros: culture of high integrity. very competent and smart people who gets things done. great pay and prestige. great Employee Resource group. safety first.
cons: the force ranking system creates bad incentives and penalize employees who have a bad year. It also forces people to be the same assertive, A-type personality to climb the ranking. It is also used unfairly to lay people off without calling it a layoff. No work life balance. I quote from a former supervisor "ExxonMobil load up your plate to 120%, and you have to decide is most important to get done". For me that often means stay in the office after hours to catch up. My decision to say no and leave the office at 4 to have more time with the family did not help my ranking.

title: Deadlines and Layoffs
pros: Salary and benefits were good
cons: Management views everyone at the lower level as just a number and dollar sign. Our managers rotated every 2-3 years from other departments, so we were ranked for career progression by people with zero years of applicable experience.

title: Compensate Poor Management with High Pay
body: Compensate Poor Management with High Pay
pros: Very high pay. Great benefits. Great coworkers.
cons: Dangerous if working in a plant. Upper management is so disconnected from the lower level work that horrible decisions by middle management are causing employees to flee. They are sending many jobs overseas to be preformed remotely and have no problem cutting employees without warning. The ranking system preformed every year is entirely subjective and at the mercy of the manager. Employees have no say or option to appeal a poor score.
